summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Application made for the parents to contribute to the costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer – Where the mother would suffer financial hardship – Orders made for the father to pay half of the costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SIMPSON J:

These parenting proceedings concluded on the final day of a five day hearing when the parties reached agreement. Final parenting orders were made that day by consent (“the final orders”) which provide that the children will continue to live with the mother and spend no time with, nor communicate with, the father.
The final orders included an order that the father would pay costs to the mother, in a fixed amount, and provided for the sale of real property in the event of the father’s failure to pay those costs. The final orders made were consistent with the final orders sought by the mother.
Counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) made an application that the parties contribute to the costs of the ICL. The mother was privately represented in these proceedings. The father was assisted by legal representatives under the s 102NA Family Violence and Cross-Examination of Parties Scheme.
The ICL had filed a Costs Notice on 27 January 2026, which indicated total costs incurred in the amount of $32,186. Each of the parents opposed the orders sought on behalf of the ICL that they should each contribute to 50 per cent of the fees of the ICL, which I was advised equalled $16,093 per parent.
Section 114UC of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) provides as follows:
114UC Costs of independent children’s lawyer and limitations on costs relating to intervening officer or litigation guardian etc.
(1) In proceedings in which an independent children’s lawyer for a child has been appointed, the court may make a costs order, whether by way of interlocutory order or otherwise, to the effect that each party to the proceedings bears, in such proportion as the court considers just, the costs of the independent children’s lawyer in relation to the proceedings.

(2) However, if:
(a) a party to the proceedings is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings; or

(b) the court considers that a party to the proceedings would suffer financial hardship if the party had to bear a proportion of the costs of the independent children’s lawyer;

the court must not make a costs order against that party in relation to the costs of the independent children’s lawyer.
(3) For the purposes of paragraph (2)(a), assistance by way of legal aid does not include assistance provided to a party in accordance with a Commonwealth scheme operating for the purpose of applying the requirements of subsection 102NA(2).

...
It was submitted on behalf of the mother that the making of the order as sought by counsel for the ICL would cause the mother to suffer financial hardship. The mother remains responsible for the care of the parties’ two children. As a consequence of the final orders, the children will spend no time with the father such that the mother is entirely responsible for meeting their day to day care needs.
It was not controversial that the father was paying child support to the mother, in accordance with an administrative assessment, of approximately $22 per week. The mother’s Costs Notice filed on 30 January 2026 (Exhibit 36), and as confirmed in submissions, revealed that the mother has expended more than $430,000 on legal fees and associated costs (including expert witness costs).
It was submitted on behalf of the mother that the costs of the ICL should be met by the father in circumstances where the mother had made an offer to settle the matter, a week prior to the final hearing, on terms which were the same as those ultimately agreed by the parties on the final day of the hearing. Counsel for the mother submitted accordingly that, had the father accepted the offer as put by the mother, the final hearing would not have occurred and at least that portion of the ICL’s costs would have been avoided.
As noted above, the final orders included an order that the father pay some of the mother’s costs, which amount represented only a portion of the total costs incurred by the mother over the entirety of the proceedings.
More generally, as to the financial circumstances of the mother, I accept that at the time of the final hearing, the mother was not in employment, having been made redundant in November 2025. The mother indicated in her oral evidence that her ability to consider future employment was also impacted by her focus on the final hearing and management of her own health while continuing to care for the children. I accept the mother’s evidence that she was at the time of the hearing, without income from employment outside of the home.
It was submitted on behalf of the father that he was also experiencing financial difficulties and that the making of the costs order as sought on behalf of the ICL would cause the father financial hardship. The reality of the father’s financial situation remained unclear. The father’s evidence was entirely unsatisfactory. The father’s evidence was the subject of substantial challenge, and the father made admissions as to being untruthful in parts of his evidence. The father admitted to the fabrication of documents (in relation to his financial circumstances). It would be difficult to accept any part of the father’s evidence (where contradicted by evidence given by the mother) if not corroborated by other evidence from a reliable source.
Despite those circumstances, I was still invited to accept that the father’s income, as reported by him, was modest. I was also invited to consider the extent of borrowing undertaken by the father (about which there was some independent third-party evidence) and the additional financial obligation to the mother, to which he had agreed in the final orders.
I am not persuaded that the father’s failure to accept the mother’s offer prior to the commencement of the final hearing should result in him also taking responsibility for all of the costs incurred in these proceedings by the ICL. The mother has secured a costs remedy against the father in the final orders and that was resolved by agreement between them. To make the father also responsible for the mother’s notional share of the ICL’s costs is akin to expanding the costs order, already agreed between the parties. I decline to do so.
However, I accept the submissions on behalf of the mother that it would cause the mother financial hardship if she was ordered to contribute to the costs of the ICL and I decline to make that order.
I am not prepared to accept that the father’s circumstances are such that it would cause financial hardship to him if he was ordered to contribute to the costs of the ICL. I simply do not have confidence about any evidence given by the father and the father’s true financial position was entirely unclear.
I order that the father shall contribute to 50 per cent of the fees of the ICL.
